text: reviewed and translated much of this European literature. Arnold van Gennep probably was not what one would call an ‘easy person’. From his early youth he gained a reputation for doing things his own way. He initially enrolled in a school in Lyons, but due to numerous behavioural problems he was transferred to Sainte-Barbe-des Champs in Paris in 1884. He completed high school in Nice, receiving prizes for his grades, but also penalties for frequent misconduct. During his time in Nice he learned English, and also took lessons in Italian and Spanish. He then taught himself Portuguese. He excelled in most subjects, except mathematics, for which he never developed a passion. In 1892, van Gennep received the philo aux sciences restreintes degree from a lycée in Grenoble. His adoptive father pressured him to study surgery, but Van Gennep refused, and eventually chose to start a career in diplomacy. Van Gennep then moved to Paris. He decided that the Sorbonne did not offer what he was looking for and enrolled at l’Ecole des langues orientales to study Arabic and at l’École pratique des Hautes Études, to study general linguistics and Egyptology. He also enrolled for lectures at the section of sciences religieuses in that same school, where he studied primitive religion and Islamic culture. Among others, he here came into contact with the great linguist, Antoine Meillet, a member of the Durkheim circle, and got to know Marcel Mauss as well. Mauss was just one year older than van Gennep, and they shared identical interests. In this period, van Gennep published his first articles on numismatics. His very first publication dates from 1894: a two page commentary on Merovingian coins in Revue Numismatique. He had been collecting coins from his early youth, a passion he retained throughout his life. His publications from 1894–1897 were all on numismatics. Van Gennep had also developed a strong interest in animal branding and property marks since his stays in Savoy. These interests brought him into contact with ethnography. He started to develop very ambitious academic plans, seeking out a synthesis between linguistics, ethnography and ancient history. As he wrote in 1895, at the age of 22, and still an under-graduate, his goal would be to ‘revise theories on taboo and take up again, on completely new foundations, the question of the origin of languages and alphabets’. 5 When he 5 In his own words, ‘reviser toute la théorie du tabou; à reprendre sur des bases nouvelles la question de l’origine des écritures et des alphabets [...] l’idée que la marque crée un lieu d’appartenance et de parenté; à examiner quelle est la situation exacte, par Liminality and the Modern 32 assembled his academic curriculum for a job application in 1911, he restated the centrality of property marks and signs, as such a study would allow him to ‘revise the whole theory of taboo; to reconstruct the entire question of the origin of writing and alphabets on new foundations; to conduct research into the exact meaning of tattooing, body painting, and all mutilations implying that such marks create a membership and kinship bond’ (as quoted and translated by Belmont 1979: 105). We must appreciate van Gennep’s interest in numismatics and in property marks as much more than a mere anecdote and for reasons that can be spelled out from the outset: essentially, the ‘stamping’ of liquid forms into a ‘hard’ or ‘frozen’ still-image is of course very basically what happens with coinage and with the invention of alphabetical writing; but such techniques have their close parallels in social processes of ‘stamping’ liminal experiences onto the neophyte and his or her cohort, producing lasting forms, and closing the liminal period. It is of utmost importance that Weber, in his genealogical approach, emphatically used the concept of ‘stamping’ (prägung) to designate the process whereby certain ideas or practices, initially identifiable within specific social groups, become distilled and established as lasting forms within wider populations. The question that van Gennep somehow intuited from an early age concerns how ritual and symbolic forms can leave lasting marks on both individuals and societies undergoing such ritual experiences. The ‘marque’ stands as the physical, concrete sign that centrally links the spheres of religion and taboo, belonging and kinship, language and writing. And rituals are played out in the tension between form and content. Marriage, Flight from France and Return to Paris The situation was this: Arnold van Gennep was an extremely talented and productive young man finding himself in Paris in a period where ideas and entire university disciplines were in the making. He graduated in 1896 at the Paris School of Oriental Languages. He had also kicked off his career as translator. His first two translations were an article on Islamic law from Dutch to French, published in the prestigious Revue de l’Histoire des Religions, and Frazer’s immensely influential book on totemism, both of which were printed in 1898. 6 rapport aux langues proprement dites ou communes, des langues specials’.As quoted in the short and precise biography posted online by the Musée d’ethnographie Neuchâtel at http://www.men.ch/infomusee.asp/1-3-418-99-1520-99-5-4-1 (last accessed May 4 2013). Arnold van Gennep made a lasting impact at the ethnographic museum at Neuchâtel which he led from 1912–1915. 6 Durkheim had himself hoped to be in charge of that important translation, the reference work over all others for any comparative discussion on totemism and religion. In spring 1898, while Mauss was in London to study the British school of anthropology, Durkheim asked him to approach Frazer with this translation in mind – but too late, for the young van Gennep had already been offered the job (with A. Dirr). On May 10, 1898, Durkheim writes, seemingly rather annoyed, to Mauss: ‘My project to have Totemism translated has gone down the drain. [...] You can tell Frazer that my intention was to have it Arnold van Gennep: Fragments of a Life-Work at the Thresholds 33 Translation and non-academic publication would remain van Gennep’s main source of income for much of his
